mnemonics: PASS: Parameters Are Shared Secrets (Objects are passed by reference, not copied)
keyPoints: In Java, objects are passed to methods by reference, meaning the method receives a copy of the reference to the object, not a copy of the object itself.
Changes made to the object's state inside the method persist after the method call, as the method is operating on the original object.
Primitive types (int, char, boolean, etc.) are passed by value, meaning the method receives a copy of the actual data.
commonMistakes: Assuming that passing an object to a method creates a separate copy, leading to unintended side-effects when the original object is modified.
Forgetting that the reference itself is passed by value, so reassigning the parameter to a new object inside the method does not affect the original caller's reference.
